title: XEQT vs VEQT: Which All-Equity ETF Should Canadian Investors Pick in 2026?
description: We compared 12 all-equity and equity-focused ETFs for Canadian investors. XEQT (iShares) and VEQT (Vanguard) are the two dominant players—both track global markets with minimal fees. XEQT wins slightly on size and lower MER, but VEQT just cut its fee and offers comparable performance. For most Canadians in a TFSA or RRSP, either is an excellent choice. If you want to build your own portfolio, US-listed ETFs like ITOT + IEFA + IEMG in an RRSP offer lower costs and better tax treatment.
verdict: **XEQT is the clear winner for 95% of Canadian investors. Score: 9/10.** At C$14.81B in AUM with a 0.20% MER (TradingView, BlackRock Canada), XEQT is the largest, cheapest, and most liquid all-equity ETF in Canada. It returned 22.07% over the past year (StockAnalysis), offers global diversification (25% Canada, 45% US, 25% developed international, 5% emerging markets), and requires zero maintenance in a TFSA or RRSP. VEQT is equally strong (nearly identical performance, recent fee cut), but XEQT's size and lower current cost give it a slight edge. If you're just starting out, buy XEQT, set up automatic monthly contributions, and forget about it for 10 years. Yes, embedded US withholding taxes exist in a TFSA, but the simplicity and low cost far outweigh the complexity of DIY US-listed building blocks. For tax-savvy RRSP investors who want to squeeze every basis point, US-listed ITOT + IEFA + IEMG cost less (0.06% blended), but that requires discipline, rebalancing, and currency conversions—not worth it for most people. **Would I tell my friend to buy XEQT? Absolutely. Every time.**
tags: Best All-Equity ETF for Canadians in 2026 — XEQT vs 11 Alternatives

content.headline: XEQT vs VEQT: Which All-Equity ETF Should Canadian Investors Pick in 2026?
content.summary: We compared 12 all-equity and equity-focused ETFs for Canadian investors. XEQT (iShares) and VEQT (Vanguard) are the two dominant players—both track global markets with minimal fees. XEQT wins slightly on size and lower MER, but VEQT just cut its fee and offers comparable performance. For most Canadians in a TFSA or RRSP, either is an excellent choice. If you want to build your own portfolio, US-listed ETFs like ITOT + IEFA + IEMG in an RRSP offer lower costs and better tax treatment.

candidates.reasonConsidered: VEQT has C$12.2B in assets ([TipRanks](https://www.tipranks.com/etf/veqt-to)), just cut its management fee from 0.22% to 0.17% in Nov 2025 ([Vanguard Canada](https://www.vanguard.ca/en/advisor/products/products-group/etfs/VEQT)), and returned 21.77% over the past year ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/quote/tsx/VEQT/)). The fee cut hasn't flowed through to the MER yet (still 0.24%), but it will drop to ~0.19-0.20% at the next fiscal year-end. Long-term performance is strong: 13.73% over 3 years and 13.61% annualized over 5 years ([Vanguard factsheet](https://www.vanguard.ca/en/advisor/products/products-group/etfs/VEQT)).
candidates.reasonEliminated: VEQT's MER (0.24%) is slightly higher than XEQT's (0.20%) as of March 2026, and it trades at a slightly higher P/E multiple (21.99 vs 21.43), meaning you're paying a modest premium while the fee cut benefit hasn't fully materialized yet.

candidates.reasonConsidered: ZEQT offers competitive 0.20% MER ([BMO ETF Facts](https://www.bmogam.com/ca-en/products/exchange-traded-funds/bmo-all-equity-etf-zeqt/)) and a unique differentiator: dedicated mid-cap (ZMID: 2.55%) and small-cap (ZSML: 1.18%) US sleeves that neither XEQT nor VEQT include ([BMO factsheet](https://www.bmogam.com/ca-en/products/exchange-traded-funds/bmo-all-equity-etf-zeqt/)). Returned 20.47% in the past year ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/quote/tsx/ZEQT/)) with 13.7% annualized since inception (Dec 31, 2025) ([BMO ETF Facts](https://www.bmogam.com/ca-en/products/exchange-traded-funds/bmo-all-equity-etf-zeqt/)).
candidates.reasonEliminated: ZEQT has only C$450.6M in AUM ([BMO](https://www.bmogam.com/ca-en/products/exchange-traded-funds/bmo-all-equity-etf-zeqt/))—15x smaller than XEQT—creating liquidity and longevity concerns. It also trails XEQT and VEQT on 1-year returns (20.47% vs 22-22.1%), and the added complexity of mid/small-cap US tilts doesn't justify the trade-off for most buy-and-hold investors.

candidates.company: Global X All-Equity Asset Allocation ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: HEQT offers a growth/tech tilt (overweights Nasdaq-100 and Russell 2000) and returned 19.74% over the past year ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/quote/tsx/HEQT/)) with a lower P/E (18.34) than XEQT or VEQT ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/etfs/horizons-all-equity-asset-allocation)). AUM of C$321.9M ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/etfs/horizons-all-equity-asset-allocation)) is small but acceptable for an alternative.
candidates.reasonEliminated: HEQT's swap-based structure can generate phantom taxable distributions in non-registered accounts, and its heavier tech/growth tilt increases concentration risk compared to pure market-cap weighting. Returns trail XEQT by ~2.3%, making the added complexity hard to justify.

candidates.reasonConsidered: FEQT has C$4.088B in AUM ([Fidelity Canada](https://www.fidelity.ca/en/products/funds/FEQT/)), zero management fee ([Fidelity Canada](https://www.fidelity.ca/en/products/funds/FEQT/)), and impressive returns: 15.54% annualized since inception, 29.52% in 2024 ([Fundlibrary](https://www.fundlibrary.com/)). The factor tilts (low volatility, value, quality, momentum) plus ~2.4% Bitcoin allocation may provide upside during market stress.
candidates.reasonEliminated: FEQT's 0.43% MER is roughly 2x XEQT's (0.20%), and the Bitcoin exposure adds unpredictable volatility and complexity that most passive investors don't want. Factor tilts are actively managed and may underperform cap-weight; recent outperformance doesn't guarantee future returns. The higher fee cost alone disqualifies it for core holdings.

candidates.company: Global X Enhanced All-Equity Asset Allocation ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: HEQL offers 1.25x leveraged equity exposure and returned 22.39% in the past year ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/quote/tsx/HEQL/)).
candidates.reasonEliminated: HEQL's AUM is only ~C$9.5M ([ETF Portfolio Blueprint](https://www.etfportfolioblueprint.com/))—a critical red flag with real closure risk. The 1.84% MER ([ETF Portfolio Blueprint](https://www.etfportfolioblueprint.com/)) reflects embedded borrowing costs and is not comparable to unleveraged ETFs. Leverage amplifies losses during downturns, and the tiny fund size makes trading difficult.

candidates.reasonConsidered: MIX offers 1.25x leverage and was just launched in Apr 2025 with C$18.05M AUM ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/etfs/hamilton-enhanced-mixed-asset)). Current management fee is 0% until Apr 30, 2026, then 0.35% ([Hamilton ETFs](https://hamiltonetfs.com/etf/mix/)).
candidates.reasonEliminated: MIX is not an all-equity ETF—it holds ~60% US equities, ~20% bonds, and ~20% gold, then applies 1.25x leverage. This is a leveraged multi-asset strategy, not a pure equity bet. It's brand new, has minimal AUM, and the fee waiver expires in ~1 year. Not suitable for long-term buy-and-hold investors.

candidates.company: iShares Core MSCI All Country World ex Canada Index ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: XAW is a low-cost building block (0.22% MER) ([BlackRock Canada](https://www.blackrock.com/ca/investors/en/products/272108/ishares-core-msci-all-country-world-ex-canada-index-etf)) with C$3.77B in AUM ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/TSX-XAW/)) and returned 15.96% over the past year ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/quote/tsx/XAW/)). Ideal for DIY builders who pair it with XIC for a Canadian/global split.
candidates.reasonEliminated: XAW excludes Canada (0% allocation), so it's not a complete all-in-one solution—you'd need to add Canadian equities separately. For investors wanting simplicity, XEQT or VEQT are easier one-ticket solutions.

candidates.company: iShares Core S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: XIC is Canada's cheapest equity ETF with a 0.06% MER ([BlackRock Canada](https://www.blackrock.com/ca/investors/en/products/239837/ishares-sptsx-capped-composite-index-etf)), C$26.25B in AUM ([TipRanks](https://www.tipranks.com/etf/xic-to)), and a 2.08% distribution yield ([Investing.com](https://www.investing.com/etfs/ishares-s-p-tsx-capped-composite)). Ideal for TFSA investors seeking Canadian dividend tax credit and no FX conversion.
candidates.reasonEliminated: XIC is 100% Canadian equity, so it's too concentrated for global investors. Heavy tilt toward financials (~35%) and energy (~15%) creates sector-specific risk. Not suitable as a standalone all-equity global portfolio—only as a Canadian sleeve or core Canadian holding.

candidates.company: iShares Core S&P Total U.S. Stock Market ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: ITOT is the gold standard for US equity exposure: 0.03% MER ([iShares](https://www.ishares.com/us/products/239724/ishares-core-sp-total-us-stock-market-etf)), US$82.4B AUM ([Dividend.com](https://www.dividend.com/etfs/itot-ishares-core-s-p-total-u-s-stock-market-etf/)), and returned 18.2% over the past year ([Dividend.com](https://www.dividend.com/etfs/itot-ishares-core-s-p-total-u-s-stock-market-etf/)). Morningstar Gold Medalist ([Morningstar](https://www.morningstar.com/)). In an RRSP, the Canada-US tax treaty avoids 15% US withholding tax on dividends.
candidates.reasonEliminated: ITOT is US-only and trades in USD, so it's a building block, not an all-in-one solution. Requires currency conversion and RRSP-specific optimization. For investors wanting simplicity in a TFSA, XEQT or VEQT are vastly easier.

candidates.reasonConsidered: IEFA covers developed international markets (Europe, Australasia, Far East) with a 0.07% MER ([iShares](https://www.ishares.com/us/products/286762/ishares-core-msci-eafe-etf)), US$171.4B AUM ([bestetf.net](https://www.bestetf.net/)), and returned 21.07% over the past year ([bestetf.net](https://www.bestetf.net/)). Higher dividend yield (~3.45%) than US-focused ETFs ([bestetf.net](https://www.bestetf.net/)). In an RRSP, avoids some US withholding tax layers.
candidates.reasonEliminated: IEFA covers only developed international, not emerging markets or US equities. Multi-layer withholding taxes (L1 country + L2 US) remain complex even in an RRSP. Standalone, it's a building block, not a complete portfolio.

candidates.company: iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: IEMG offers broad emerging market exposure (US$135.5B+ AUM) ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/IEMG/)) with exceptional recent returns: 33-37.5% over the past year ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/IEMG/), [StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/etf/iemg/)) and 18.9% annualized over 3 years ([bestetf.net](https://www.bestetf.net/)). Uniquely includes South Korea (Taiwan Semiconductor, Samsung, Tencent are top holdings), with a 0.09% MER ([iShares](https://www.ishares.com/us/products/244050/ishares-core-msci-emerging-markets-etf)). YTD 2026: +7.08% ([bestetf.net](https://www.bestetf.net/)).
candidates.reasonEliminated: IEMG is emerging markets only and carries the highest withholding tax complexity (multi-layer L1 country + L2 US). Recent outperformance (+33-37.5%) is exceptional and unlikely to repeat; valuation may be stretched. For core holdings, it should be a 5-10% sleeve, not a standalone vehicle.

winner.reasonConsidered: XEQT is the largest and most liquid all-equity ETF in Canada: C$14.81B in AUM ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/TSX-XEQT/)) with 513,776 average daily volume ([Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/XEQT.TO/)). It returned 22.07% over the past year ([StockAnalysis](https://stockanalysis.com/quote/tsx/XEQT/)) with a 0.20% MER ([BlackRock Canada](https://www.blackrock.com/ca/investors/en/products/309480/ishares-core-equity-etf-portfolio)) and Morningstar Medalist rating ([Morningstar](https://www.morningstar.com/)). The global allocation (Canada ~25% | US ~45% | Intl Developed ~25% | EM ~5%) balances growth and stability. It trades on the TSX in CAD, so there's no FX conversion hassle in a TFSA. Quarterly distributions (1.64% yield) ([TradingView](https://www.tradingview.com/symbols/TSX-XEQT/)) provide flexibility. For 95% of Canadian investors seeking a simple, one-ticket solution, XEQT is the obvious choice.

content.analysis:
## Head-to-Head: XEQT vs VEQT (The Two Contenders)

| Metric | XEQT | VEQT | Winner | Plain English |
|--------|------|------|--------|---------------|
| **AUM** | C$14.81B | C$12.18B | XEQT | Larger fund = better liquidity, lower closure risk, economies of scale |
| **MER** | 0.20% | 0.24% → ~0.19-0.20% expected | XEQT (today); VEQT (soon) | MER is the total all-in cost. VEQT's fee cut hasn't flowed through yet, but will by mid-2026 |
| **Management Fee** | 0.17% (cut Dec 2025) | 0.17% (cut Nov 2025) | Tie | Both providers cut fees within a month of each other — now identical |
| **1-Year Return** | 22.07% | 21.77% | XEQT | Both performed nearly identically; XEQT slightly ahead |
| **5Y Annualized** | 14.00% | 13.61% | XEQT | XEQT edges out VEQT slightly over 5 years with a marginally stronger return |
| **Price** | C$40.59 | C$54.60 | Neutral | Different price = different unit sizes; not comparable directly |
| **P/E Ratio** | 21.43 | 21.99 | XEQT | Lower P/E = cheaper valuation, less frothy |
| **Daily Volume** | 513,776 | 308,363 | XEQT | Easier to buy/sell without moving the market |
| **Distribution Yield** | 1.64% | 1.35% | XEQT | XEQT pays slightly higher distributions |
| **Distribution Frequency** | Quarterly | Annually | XEQT | Quarterly = more flexibility; annual = simpler tax reporting |
| **Allocation** | Canada 25%, US 45%, Dev Intl 25%, EM 5% | Canada 30%, US ~44%, Dev Intl ~20%, EM ~6% | XEQT | XEQT is more US-heavy; VEQT tilts slightly more Canada/EM. Difference is minimal (~4-5%). |
| **Morningstar Rating** | Medalist | N/A shown | XEQT | Rating indicates quality and consistency |

**Key Takeaway:** As of March 2026, XEQT edges out VEQT on cost (0.20% vs 0.24% MER), size (C$14.8B vs C$12.2B), and liquidity. However, VEQT's recent fee cut (Nov 2025) will narrow the gap to ~0.01% by mid-year. The choice between them is not huge—both are excellent.

## Why XEQT Wins

XEQT is the **largest, cheapest, and most liquid** all-equity ETF in Canada. With C$14.81B in AUM and 513,776 average daily trades, you'll never have trouble buying or selling (TradingView [1], Yahoo Finance [2]). The 0.20% MER means you keep 99.8¢ of every dollar; VEQT's 0.24% costs slightly more, though this gap will narrow after its fee cut flows through.

Performance is nearly identical (XEQT: 22.07% vs VEQT: 21.77% over 1 year) (StockAnalysis [3], StockAnalysis [4]), so you're not sacrificing returns. The 1.64% distribution yield and quarterly payouts (TradingView [1]) offer flexibility if you need income—VEQT pays annually, which is simpler for taxes but less flexible.

For a first-time investor in a TFSA or RRSP, XEQT is **set-it-and-forget-it:** one ticker, global diversification (25% Canada, 45% US, 25% developed international, 5% emerging markets), and zero thinking required. At C$40.59 per unit (Yahoo Finance [2]), it's also accessible to beginners.

## The Runner-Up: VEQT

VEQT is nearly indistinguishable from XEQT and would be my second choice. It has a slightly longer track record (inception Jan 2019 vs XEQT's Aug 2019) and just cut its management fee from 0.22% to 0.17% in November 2025 (Vanguard Canada [5]). Once the MER update flows through, VEQT's all-in cost will drop to ~0.19-0.20%, matching or beating XEQT. VEQT's slightly higher EM tilt (6.8% vs 5%) and Canada tilt (30% vs 25%) may appeal to investors who want more Canadian exposure. However, it trades at a higher P/E (21.99 vs 21.43) and has lower daily volume (308,363 vs 513,776), making it slightly less liquid. If XEQT is the "Coke," VEQT is the "Pepsi"—choose either and you'll be fine.

## Alternative Strategies

For investors comfortable with more complexity, **DIY builders can save money** by combining US-listed ETFs in an RRSP: ITOT (0.03% MER, US$82.4B AUM) + IEFA (0.07% MER, US$171.4B AUM) + IEMG (0.09% MER, US$135.5B AUM) achieve an all-in cost of ~0.06% blended. The Canada-US tax treaty avoids 15% withholding tax on US dividends in an RRSP, offsetting ITOT's lower yield (1.33% vs XEQT's 1.64%). However, this requires annual rebalancing, currency conversions, and tax reporting discipline—not ideal for beginners.
risks: Both XEQT and VEQT hold US and international equities which incur Level 2 withholding tax on dividends even in TFSAs (unavoidable for TSX-listed ETFs); RRSP is tax-advantaged but TFSA still works fine for long-term buy-and-hold investors.
Market correction: Both ETFs carry ~1.0 beta, meaning they'll move with the market; a 20-30% pullback would hurt as much as the market itself—this is normal for all-equity products.
VEQT's MER hasn't updated yet to reflect its November 2025 fee cut; expect updated documentation by mid-2026, but the current 0.24% is inflated and will drop.
XEQT's P/E of 21.43 suggests moderate valuation; US equities trade at ~26.88 P/E (ITOT), implying some US market stretch—neither is a screaming bargain.
Neither XEQT nor VEQT include Canada's small-caps or emerging growth stories (e.g., Shopify, Docebo); both focus on mega-cap stability.
Currency risk: Weak CAD could boost USD-denominated returns; strong CAD would drag them. Holding CAD-listed ETFs (XEQT, VEQT) avoids direct FX conversion but you still own US assets.
catalysts: Upcoming earnings season (Q1 2026): Tech-heavy US indices (45% of XEQT) will report; strong earnings could drive valuations higher or trigger profit-taking.

VEQT's MER update (mid-2026): Once the November 2025 fee cut officially updates the MER from 0.24% to ~0.19-0.20%, VEQT may become marginally cheaper than XEQT, creating a brief rebalancing opportunity.

Federal Reserve policy: If interest rates fall, bond prices rise and equities benefit; if rates stay high or rise further, growth stocks could face headwinds (both ETFs are 100% equity with no bond cushion).
Emerging market strength: IEMG's 33-37% 1-year return shows EM is hot; if China or India accelerate, XEQT's 5% EM sleeve could outperform—small but meaningful upside.
Canadian oil and bank sector: XIC shows financials (~35%) and energy (~15%) dominate the TSX; XEQT's 25% Canada tilt benefits if commodity prices stabilize or rates fall.

dataPoints.label: VEQT MER
dataPoints.value: 0.24% (expected to drop to ~0.19-0.20% after fee cut flows through)

content.finalVerdict: **XEQT is the clear winner for 95% of Canadian investors. Score: 9/10.** At C$14.81B in AUM with a 0.20% MER (TradingView [1], BlackRock Canada [6]), XEQT is the largest, cheapest, and most liquid all-equity ETF in Canada. It returned 22.07% over the past year (StockAnalysis [3]), offers global diversification (25% Canada, 45% US, 25% developed international, 5% emerging markets), and requires zero maintenance in a TFSA or RRSP. VEQT is equally strong (nearly identical performance, recent fee cut), but XEQT's size and lower current cost give it a slight edge. If you're just starting out, buy XEQT, set up automatic monthly contributions, and forget about it for 10 years. Yes, embedded US withholding taxes exist in a TFSA, but the simplicity and low cost far outweigh the complexity of DIY US-listed building blocks. For tax-savvy RRSP investors who want to squeeze every basis point, US-listed ITOT + IEFA + IEMG cost less (0.06% blended), but that requires discipline, rebalancing, and currency conversions—not worth it for most people. **Would I tell my friend to buy XEQT? Absolutely. Every time.**
